content.problemStatement:
In Java, the greater than (`>`) operator is used to compare two primitive values. It checks whether the value on the left side is greater than the value on the right side and returns a boolean result (`true` or `false`).

---

**Comparing Integers** 
The `>` operator can be used to compare integer values:
```java
int num1 = 15; // num1 holds an integer value of 15  
int num2 = 10; // num2 holds an integer value of 10  
System.out.println(num1 > num2);  // Outputs: true
```

**Comparing Floating-Point Numbers** 
The `>` operator also works with floating-point numbers (`double` and `float`):
```java
double height1 = 5.8; // height1 holds a double value of 5.8  
double height2 = 6.2; // height2 holds a double value of 6.2  
System.out.println(height1 > height2);  // Outputs: false  
```

**Comparing Characters** 
Since characters (char) are internally represented as numeric Unicode values, they can be compared using `>`:
```java
char letterA = 'A'; // letterA holds the character 'A' (Unicode 65)  
char letterB = 'B'; // letterB holds the character 'B' (Unicode 66)  
System.out.println(letterA > letterB);  // Outputs: false
```
